title: Habit tracker design
subtitle: Drawing a monthly habit tracker page by hand
excerpt: A hand-ruled habit tracker page records whether a set of daily habits were completed. It is more useful than a printed tracker because you choose exactly which habits to track and how much detail to capture per day. The design process, working out the grid, the symbols, the colour code, is itself a useful exercise in clarifying what you actually want to monitor.
sourceNotes: Habit tracker layout guidance synthesised from bullet-journal and journalling-craft practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A matrix with habits listed down the left column and days of the month along the top row. Each cell is marked when the habit is completed. A 31-day month fits on a landscape A5 page with five to eight habits comfortably.

content.text: is the simplest structured page in journalling. It is a table: habits on one axis, days on the other. The value of drawing it by hand is that you design the grid around the habits you are tracking, rather than adjusting your habits to fit a printed page.

Limit the tracker to six to eight habits per month. More than eight becomes visually cluttered and the recording overhead starts to feel like a habit in itself rather than a record of other habits. Choose habits that are binary, done or not done, rather than habits that need a numerical entry. A separate log page handles habits with graduated outcomes (distance run, pages read) more cleanly than a grid.

On an A5 landscape page with 10 mm margins: the habit name column on the left is 35, 40 mm wide. The remaining width (roughly 120 mm) is divided into 31 cells for the days. Each cell comes out at approximately 4 mm wide, small enough to fit a dot, cross, or filled square. The row height for each habit should be at least 6 mm to leave room for writing the habit name in full, and to give enough space for a legible mark in the cell.

Mark every column lightly in pencil before inking. Rule the full page in one direction first (all rows), then turn the ruler and rule all columns. Use a 0.3 mm liner for the inner grid lines and a slightly heavier 0.5 mm for the outer border and the line separating the habit column from the day cells. Washi tape applied along the borders before ruling gives a clean, coloured border that is faster than ruling and colouring separately.

A filled dot or a small filled square is the fastest mark to make in a 4 mm cell. A simple cross (X) also works. Avoid symbols that require multiple strokes or that look similar to each other, tick and cross look the same at very small sizes. If you want to record partial completion, a half-filled circle (coloured bottom half only) gives a third state at small size.
